5: heis gar theos heis kai mesites theou kai anthropon anthropos Christos Iesous (heis gar theos, heis kai mesitēs theou kai anthrōpōn, anthrōpos Christos Iēsous): 'For there is one God and one mediator between God and people, the man Christ Jesus.' The mesites (mediator) terminology was used in both Hellenistic contract law (a third party who guarantees a transaction) and in LXX usage for Moses's mediating role at Sinai (Galatians 3:19-20). The Christological uniqueness: there is one mediator — contra the Greco-Roman polytheistic model of multiple divine intermediaries, and contra any Jewish or emerging Gnostic scheme of angelic mediators. The qualifier anthrōpos (man) before Christ Jesus emphasizes the genuine humanity that enables the mediation: the mediator must be able to touch both parties.
1: parakalō oun prōton pantōn poieisthai deēseis proseukas enteuxeis eucharistias hyper pantōn anthrōpōn — 'I urge, then, first of all, that supplications, prayers, intercessions, and thanksgivings be made for all people.' The four prayer terms represent the full range of petitionary and worshipful address: deēseis (requests, needs-based petitions), proseuchas (prayers generally — the broadest term), enteuxeis (intercessions — interventions on another's behalf, related to entugchanō used of Christ's intercession in Romans 8:34), eucharistias (thanksgivings). The comprehensive scope hyper pantōn anthrōpōn (for all people) is immediately narrowed in 1 Timothy 2:2 to the practical application: kings and those in authority.
2: hyper basileōn kai pantōn tōn en hyperochē ontōn — 'for kings and all who are in high position.' The prayer for rulers is grounded in Jewish Diaspora practice (Jeremiah 29:7 — pray for the welfare of the city; Ezra 6:10; 1 Macc 1 Timothy 12:11) and early Christian convention. The goal — hinaēremon kai hēsuchion bion diagōmen (that we may live a quiet and peaceful life) — is social stability rather than political endorsement of specific rulers. Eusebeia kai semnotēs (godliness and dignity) characterize the quality of the life sought: the eusebeia-vocabulary is distinctively Pastoral, appearing 8 times in 1 Timothy alone (out of 15 NT occurrences).
3: touto kalon kai apodekton enōpion tou sōtēros hēmōn theou — 'this is good and acceptable before God our Savior.' The divine approval grounds the community practice in the character of God: God's will for universal salvation (1 Timothy 2:4) is the reason prayer for all people is fitting. Apodekton (acceptable, pleasing) — a term from the sacrificial vocabulary of things accepted at the altar. The prayer for all people is itself a kind of spiritual offering that pleases the God who wills the salvation of all.
4: hos pantas anthrōpous thelei sōthēnai kai eis epignōsin alētheias elthein — 'who desires all people to be saved and to come to a knowledge of the truth.' The universalist scope of pantas anthrōpous (all people) has generated centuries of debate: does it mean every individual without exception, or all types/categories of people? Paul's immediate context (prayer for kings, 1 Timothy 2:2) suggests he means without distinction of social rank — kings as well as commoners. Epignōsis alētheias (full knowledge of the truth) — epignōsis (precise, experiential knowledge, stronger than gnōsis) is the Pastoral Epistles' characteristic term for saving knowledge (1 Timothy 2:4; 2 Timothy 2:25; 1 Timothy 3:7; Titus 1:1). Truth here is not propositional only but relational.
6: ho dous heauton antilutron hyper pantōn — 'who gave himself as a ransom for all.' Antilutron is a Pauline hapax (this is its only NT occurrence) intensifying lutron (ransom, Mark 10:45): the anti prefix marks substitution — a ransom given in place of others. The compound emphasizes the substitutionary nature of Christ's death more explicitly than Jesus's own lutron anti pollōn (Mark 10:45). To marturion kairois idiois (the testimony at its proper times) — the gospel is the divinely appointed disclosure of what Christ's self-giving means, timed by God's sovereignty.
7: kērux kai apostolos kai didaskalos ethnōn en pistei kai alētheiā — 'herald, apostle, and teacher of the Gentiles in faith and truth.' Three functional designations: kērux (herald — the proclaimer of news, the one who announces publicly) is the most primitive image of the preacher as town-crier. Apostolos (sent one — with full authorization of the sender). Didaskalos (teacher — the one who explains and instructs). The insistence 'I am telling the truth, I am not lying' (alētheuō, ou pseudomai) is a Pauline oath-formula (cf. Romans 9:1; Galatians 1:20) inserted because this particular claim — apostle to the Gentiles — was contested by opponents.
8: boulomaioun proseuchesthai tous andras en panti topō epairontas hosias cheiras chōris orgēs kai dialogismou — 'I want the men in every place to pray, lifting holy hands without anger or disputing.' Andras here is male-specific (not anthrōpous = people generally). The posture of prayer — epairontas hosias cheiras (lifting holy hands) — was the standard Jewish and early Christian prayer posture (cf. 1 Kings 8:22; Psalms 28:2; 1 Timothy 63:4). Hosias (holy, devout) — the hands must be ritually and morally clean. The qualifiers 'without anger or disputing' suggest that prayer in the Ephesian community was being disrupted by internal conflict — the instruction addresses the social preconditions for legitimate prayer.
9: hōsautōs kai gynaikas en katastolē kosmia meta aidous kai sōphrosynēs kosmein heautas — 'likewise women to adorn themselves in respectable apparel, with modesty and self-control.' Katastolē (clothing, deportment) covers both dress and general bearing. Aidōs (modesty, a sense of shame before others) and sōphrosynē (self-control, sound-mindedness) were recognized female virtues in both Jewish and Greco-Roman culture. The specific prohibitions — plēgmasin ē chrysiō ē margaritais ē himatismō polytele (braided hair, gold, pearls, expensive clothing) — match similar prohibitions in 1 Peter 3:3 and echo Roman and Jewish moralizing literature about wealthy women's ostentation. The issue is conspicuous wealth-display in worship, not dress codes for their own sake.
10: alla ho prepei gynaixi epangellomenais theosebeian di' ergōn agathōn — 'but what is fitting for women who profess godliness — through good deeds.' The contrast: not costly adornment but good deeds is the proper self-presentation for a woman whose claim is theosebeia (reverence toward God, piety — the word is a near-synonym of eusebeia). The verb prepei (is fitting, is appropriate) invokes the category of social propriety — what is congruent with one's stated values. Claiming godliness while displaying wealth is an incongruity; embodying it through good works is consistency.
11: gunē en hēsuchia manthanetō en pasē hypotagē — 'a woman is to learn in quietness in all submission.' Hēsuchia (quietness, stillness) is not the same as sigē (silence/speechlessness) — the word connotes a settled, undisruptive disposition rather than absolute silence (cf. its use in 2 Thessalonians 3:12 for 'quietly working'). The command to learn (manthanetō) is itself counter-cultural: many Greco-Roman contexts excluded women from formal instruction. The parallel with 1 Corinthians 14:34-35 and the qualification en pasē hypotagē (in all submissiveness) — submissive to the teaching authority structure, not to all men universally.
12: didaskein de gynaiki ouk epitrepō oude authentein andros alla einai en hēsuchia — 'I do not permit a woman to teach or to exercise authority over a man; she is to remain in quietness.' This is the most contested verse in 1 Timothy. Three issues: (1) epitrepō is present active — a current directive, possibly situation-specific; (2) authentein appears only here in the NT — its semantic range is debated: 'have authority over,' 'domineer,' or even 'instigate' or 'be responsible for'; (3) the scope: all women, or the wives of male leadership? Whether the two infinitives didaskein ... authentein are joined as a hendiadys (teaching-in-an-authoritative-domineering-way) or separately is unresolved. The grounding in creation order (1 Timothy 2:13-14) has led most interpreters to see the principle as trans-situational, though the situation-specific terms of 1 Timothy 2:8 ('I want') signal this is directive, not command.
13: Adam gar prōtos eplasthē, eita Eua — 'For Adam was formed first, then Eve.' The appeal to creation order grounds the directive of 1 Timothy 2:12 in Genesis 2:7, 21-23. Eplasthē (was formed) — the aorist passive of plassō (to form, mold, as a potter), the LXX word for YHWH's forming Adam from the dust (Genesis 2:7-8). The primogeniture argument (firstborn = greater authority) is a Jewish hermeneutical principle used also by Paul in 1 Corinthians 11:8-9 and 1 Timothy 15:45-47. Whether the appeal is to creation order as permanent ontological hierarchy or to the particular situation of the fall's effects is the hermeneutical crux of the passage.
14: kai Adam ouk ēpatēthē, hē de gunē exapatēthheisa en parabasei gegonen — 'and Adam was not deceived, but the woman was deceived and became a transgressor.' Exapatēthheisa (thoroughly deceived) — the compound verb exapataō intensifies simple deception (cf. 2 Corinthians 11:3 where Paul uses the same word of the serpent's deception of Eve). The argument is not that women are inherently more susceptible to deception — Adam's sin (cf. Romans 5:12-21 where Adam bears the primary responsibility) was deliberate disobedience rather than deception. The passage uses the deception-first narrative (Genesis 3:1-6) to ground the teaching-authority principle in the fall's dynamics, not in ontological inferiority.
15: sōthēsetai de dia tēs teknogonias — 'but she will be saved through childbearing.' The most puzzling verse in 1 Timothy. Sōthēsetai could mean: (a) eschatologically saved — if so, by works of childbearing, which contradicts Pauline soteriology; (b) preserved/kept safe through the process of childbearing — a physical promise; (c) saved through the childbearing — i.e., through Christ's birth (the definite article tēs teknogonias suggests 'the childbearing'), making this a Christological reference to Genesis 3:15 (the seed of the woman). Option (c) has minority support but makes best theological sense: women are saved by the same gospel as men — through the birth of the Messiah from woman. The switch to plural 'if they remain' signals the ongoing condition for all women, not just Eve.
